request: When a contract is signed, we want an invoice to be created automatically.

problem.value: Reduce manual delay/errors in issuing invoices after contract signature; ensure billing isn't missed or duplicated.
problem.evidence: Implicit in 'we want an invoice created automatically' — no stated pain point.

current_process.evidence: Not mentioned whether invoices are currently created manually, by finance, or via another tool.

success_metrics.evidence: No KPI stated (e.g. time-to-invoice, zero missed invoices).

actors.value: Likely: whoever signs the contract (HR/ops) triggers it; finance/admin normally owns invoice creation and would now be bypassed or need to validate.
actors.evidence: Not named in request; inferred from domain roles.

business_objects.value: Contract and Invoice named explicitly. Invoice needs amount source, numbering sequence, VAT, currency, payment terms — none specified.
business_objects.evidence: Contract signed / invoice created — objects named, fields not.

business_rules.value: Unknown: does one signed contract always produce exactly one invoice, or can it be milestone/recurring/partial billing (one-to-many)? Where does the amount come from?
business_rules.evidence: Context flags contract-invoice as typically one-to-many; request implies a single 1:1 trigger.

workflow.value: Presumed trigger = contract status transition to 'signed'. Unclear if invoice creation is synchronous/blocking at signature, or async/queued; unclear initial invoice status (draft vs issued).
workflow.evidence: 'When a contract is signed' names the event but not the mechanics.

integrations.value: Who gets notified once the invoice exists (finance, client contact)? Any accounting/export system to push it to?

permissions.value: Who can see, edit or validate the auto-created invoice before it's sent? Is finance still gatekeeping?
permissions.evidence: Not addressed; permissions are a recurring blind spot per context notes.

config_vs_custom.value: Does this automatic-invoice rule apply uniformly, or vary by client, contract type, or country (billing terms, VAT rules)?
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: business rules commonly vary by client/contract/country; not addressed in request.

constraints.value: Must respect French legal invoice numbering and VAT rules per platform context.
constraints.evidence: Context card notes invoicing follows French VAT/legal numbering; not raised by client.

edge_cases.value: What if the contract is re-signed/amended (avoid duplicate invoice)? What if contract data needed for the invoice (amount, billing contact) is incomplete at signature time?
edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed; matches known trap ('create X automatically' hides idempotency and incomplete-source-data risk).

reporting.value: Traceability of auto-generated invoices (who/what triggered each) may be needed for finance audit, given legal numbering constraints.
reporting.evidence: Inferred from invoicing's regulatory sensitivity, not stated by client.

risks.value: Risk of duplicate/incorrect invoices if triggered on every signature event without idempotency control.
risks.evidence: Inferred from domain trap on automatic creation.

questions.q: Does a signed contract always produce exactly one invoice, or can it require several (milestones, recurring billing, partial invoicing)? And where should the invoice amount come from?
questions.why: High impact, empty: this is the single biggest cost driver — a 1:1 trigger and a 1:many billing engine are entirely different builds.

questions.q: What happens if the contract is re-signed via an amendment, or if required billing data (amount, billing contact, VAT info) is missing at the moment of signature?
questions.why: High impact, empty: without idempotency and incomplete-data handling, automation risks duplicate or broken invoices — a known recurring trap.

questions.q: Should invoice creation happen synchronously the instant the contract is marked signed (blocking, can fail visibly), or asynchronously in the background (queued, with a pending state)? And should the created invoice start as 'draft' or go straight to 'issued'?
questions.why: High impact, only inferred: sync vs async changes error handling and UX; draft vs issued changes who must review before the invoice is legally final.

questions.q: Does this auto-invoicing rule apply the same way for every client/contract type/country, or does it vary (e.g. different billing terms, VAT treatment)?
questions.why: High impact, empty: on a multi-client platform, a rule that varies by client is configuration, not code — changes the entire implementation approach.

questions.q: Once the invoice is auto-created, who can view, edit or must validate it before it's sent to the client — is finance still in the loop?
questions.why: High impact, empty: permissions are routinely forgotten until acceptance testing, and here they decide whether automation removes or just hides a manual check.

summary.objective: Automatically generate an invoice when a contract reaches the 'signed' state, removing manual delay and error in the billing handoff.
summary.scope: A trigger on the contract's signature event that creates an invoice pre-filled with amount, VAT, currency and legal numbering; likely starts as 'draft' pending finance validation before issuance; notification to relevant parties (finance, possibly the client's billing contact); must handle the case of amendments and incomplete source data without creating duplicates.
assumptions: One signed contract triggers the creation of one invoice by default (to be confirmed — may actually need milestone/recurring/partial billing).
Finance/admin remains involved to validate the invoice before it is sent, rather than the invoice going out fully unattended.
The trigger fires on the contract's transition to a 'signed' status, not on some other event.
The auto-created invoice starts in 'draft' status, not immediately 'issued'.
French legal invoicing rules (VAT, numbering) apply and must be respected by the automation.
summary.blind_spot: The request assumes a clean 1-to-1 mapping (one contract signed = one invoice), but contracts are commonly one-to-many with invoices (milestones, recurring billing, partial invoicing), and re-signature via an amendment could re-trigger the automation and create a duplicate invoice if idempotency isn't designed in from the start.

problem.value: Reduce manual delay/errors in billing after a contract is signed; likely a finance-ops pain point (invoices forgotten or late after signature).
problem.evidence: Request phrased as 'we want an invoice created automatically' — no stated pain, but automation intent implies a manual bottleneck today.

current_process.value: Presumably someone manually creates the invoice in the invoicing module after being told a contract was signed.
current_process.evidence: Not stated; inferred from the ask for automation.

actors.value: Likely: contract owner/sales signs, finance/admin currently creates invoice manually, finance manager may need to review before it's issued to the client.
actors.evidence: Not stated; inferred from domain (invoicing typically has a review step).

business_objects.value: Contract and Invoice are named entities; the link is 'contract signed -> invoice created'.
business_objects.evidence: "When a contract is signed, we want an invoice to be created automatically."

business_rules.value: Unknown: how is the invoice amount derived (full contract value, milestone, recurring/subscription, partial billing)? Unknown numbering, VAT, currency, payment-terms source. Unknown if the rule varies by client/contract/country.
business_rules.evidence: Context notes a Contract is one-to-many with its invoices and invoicing follows French VAT/legal numbering rules — none of this is addressed in the request.

workflow.value: Trigger = contract lifecycle event 'signed'. Unclear if an issued amendment (re-signature) should also re-trigger invoicing, and whether one invoice or a billing schedule of several is expected.
workflow.evidence: Context: Contract lifecycle draft->signed->active->ended; signature is a common automation trigger; amendments have their own draft->issued lifecycle that can re-trigger downstream automation.

integrations.value: Unknown whether invoice creation should be synchronous (blocking, fails in the signer's face) or asynchronous (queued, pending state, sync-later). Unknown who gets notified (finance? client contact?).
integrations.evidence: Context explicitly flags sync-vs-async as a recurring, unstated trap for 'when X happens, do Y' requests.

permissions.value: Unclear who can see/edit the auto-created invoice, and whether it lands as a reviewable draft or is issued directly to the client.
permissions.evidence: Not addressed; context flags permissions are 'forgotten until acceptance testing'.

config_vs_custom.value: Unknown whether this automatic-invoicing rule is universal or varies per client/contract type/country.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: 'a business rule can vary by client, contract or country' is a named recurring trap.

constraints.value: Invoice numbering and VAT must follow French legal rules; GDPR applies to any HR/contract data used.
constraints.evidence: Context: 'invoicing follows French VAT and legal numbering rules' is a hard regulatory constraint on this exact feature.

edge_cases.value: Unknown handling of: duplicate trigger firing (idempotency), incomplete billing data at signature time, contract voided/cancelled post-signature, amendment re-signature re-firing the automation.
edge_cases.evidence: Context explicitly calls out idempotency and incomplete source data as the classic 'create X automatically' trap.

reporting.value: Some audit trail of auto-created invoices (which contract triggered which invoice, when) will likely be expected given VAT/numbering obligations.
reporting.evidence: Context: invoicing is under legal numbering rules; no explicit audit ask yet, so kept at medium rather than high.

risks.value: Main risk: duplicate or incorrect invoices reaching clients if trigger/amount logic is wrong — a high-visibility failure mode.
risks.evidence: Inferred from domain sensitivity around invoicing and automation traps.

questions.q: When a contract is signed, is exactly one invoice expected, or does the contract's billing schedule (milestones, recurring/subscription, partial billing) mean several invoices over time — and if so, where does each amount, date and payment term come from?
questions.why: high impact x high uncertainty: a contract is one-to-many with its invoices in this system; 'invoice the contract' is rarely a single document, and guessing wrong reshapes the whole data model.

questions.q: Should invoice creation happen synchronously and block the signature action if something is wrong, or asynchronously (queued, with a visible pending state and retry/sync logic)? And what happens if contract data needed for the invoice (price, billing contact, VAT details) is incomplete at the moment of signature?
questions.why: high impact x high uncertainty: sync vs async is a different architecture entirely, and incomplete-source-data handling is a named recurring trap for 'create X automatically' requests.

questions.q: Does this auto-invoicing rule apply identically to every client/contract type/country, or does it vary (e.g. different VAT regimes, different billing triggers per client)?
questions.why: high impact x high uncertainty: business rules that vary by client/contract/country change this from a single hardcoded automation to a configurable rule engine.

questions.q: Once auto-created, does the invoice need human review/approval before being issued to the client, or is it sent out directly? Who should be able to see or edit it in the meantime?
questions.why: high impact x high uncertainty: permissions are typically forgotten until acceptance testing, and the answer decides if this is a draft-and-review workflow or a straight-through process.

questions.q: If a signed contract is later amended (a new issued amendment re-opens signature), should that re-trigger invoice creation, and if a signature event fires twice by mistake, how do we guarantee we don't create duplicate invoices?
questions.why: high impact x high uncertainty: amendments have their own signature-like event, and idempotency on the trigger is a classic unstated requirement that breaks naive automations.

summary.objective: Automatically create an invoice when a contract is signed, removing the manual step and delay currently required to bill a client after signature.
summary.scope: A trigger on the contract's 'signed' event that generates an invoice (or invoices, if billing is milestone-based/recurring) with amount, numbering, VAT and payment terms populated from the contract; likely routed through a review step before being issued, with an audit trail linking contract to invoice(s).
assumptions: There is currently a manual invoice-creation step that this automation replaces.
Finance/admin creates the invoice today and may need a review step before it's sent.
The contract-to-invoice link may be one-to-many rather than a single document.
An issued amendment's re-signature may need to re-trigger invoicing.
Some audit trail of auto-created invoices will be expected for compliance/traceability.
summary.blind_spot: The request assumes 'signed -> invoice' is a single, simple action, but a contract is one-to-many with its invoices (milestones, recurring billing, partial billing), the trigger's sync/async nature and idempotency are undefined, and whether the rule is universal or varies by client/contract/country is unknown — any of these can turn a simple automation into a rule engine or a source of duplicate/incorrect invoices reaching clients.

problem.value: Reduce delay/manual effort in billing after a contract is signed, and avoid missed or late invoices.
problem.evidence: Client states desired automation ('invoice created automatically') but not the underlying pain (delay, errors, missed billing).

current_process.evidence: Not stated — likely manual invoice creation today by an admin/finance person after signature.

actors.value: System triggers creation; finance/admin likely reviews or issues the invoice; contract owner may be notified.
actors.evidence: Not explicit — inferred from context (invoice lifecycle requires human validation before issuance).

business_objects.value: Contract and Invoice are the two objects involved. Amount, VAT, numbering, payment terms, currency must be sourced from the contract or elsewhere.
business_objects.evidence: Explicit: 'contract signed' triggers 'invoice created'. Fields/source of invoice data not specified.

business_rules.value: Unclear whether one invoice covers the full contract, or the contract generates several invoices (milestones, recurring, partial billing).
business_rules.evidence: Context: Contract is one-to-many with Invoice; 'invoice the contract' is rarely a single document.

workflow.value: Contract signature (event) triggers invoice creation, likely in draft state pending review/issuance. Trigger type (sync/async) and idempotency undefined.
workflow.evidence: Context: signature is a common trigger for downstream automation; 'create X automatically' hides trigger type, idempotency, incomplete-data handling.

integrations.value: Possible notification to finance/client contact, or push to accounting system.

permissions.evidence: Not stated — who can see/edit/validate the auto-created invoice before it's issued is undefined.

config_vs_custom.evidence: Not stated whether this rule is standard for all clients/contracts or configurable per client/contract type.

constraints.value: French VAT and legal invoice numbering rules apply.
constraints.evidence: Context: invoicing follows French VAT and legal numbering rules — platform-wide constraint, not explicitly raised by client.

edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed: contract amended after signature (re-triggers?), cancelled/voided contract, incomplete contract data (missing price/dates), duplicate signature events.

reporting.evidence: No compliance/audit need explicitly named beyond standard invoice legal requirements.

risks.value: Risk of duplicate invoice creation, invoicing with wrong/incomplete amounts, or invoices created for contracts that shouldn't be billed yet.
risks.evidence: Inferred from context's recurring traps around automatic creation.

questions.q: Does one signed contract always produce exactly one invoice, or can it require several (milestones, recurring/subscription billing, partial invoicing)?
questions.why: high impact x high uncertainty: this decides the whole data model — a single invoice-creation script vs. a billing schedule engine. Contract is one-to-many with Invoice per context.

questions.q: Where does the invoice amount, VAT rate, payment terms and currency come from — fixed contract fields, a pricing table, or manual entry after auto-creation?
questions.why: high impact x high uncertainty: an invoice is 'rarely just created' — without a defined amount source the automation can't run at all.

questions.q: Should the invoice be created in draft (pending finance review) or issued immediately and irreversibly? And what happens if the same signature event fires twice, or a signed contract is later amended?
questions.why: high impact x high uncertainty: sync-vs-async trigger, idempotency and amendment handling are classic traps that change error handling and architecture, not just a rule tweak.

questions.q: Who is allowed to see, edit or validate the auto-created invoice before it is sent to the client?
questions.why: high impact x empty: permissions are typically forgotten until acceptance testing, and an auto-created financial document needs a clear owner.

questions.q: Is this automatic-invoicing rule the same for every client/contract type, or does it vary (e.g. some clients need manual approval, others different billing rules)?
questions.why: high impact x empty: on this configurable multi-client platform, a rule that varies by client changes this from a hardcoded script to a configurable engine.

questions.q: What should happen if the contract's data is incomplete or inconsistent at signature time (missing price, missing billing contact) — block creation, create a flagged draft, or notify someone?
questions.why: high impact x empty: named recurring trap for 'create X automatically' — undefined behavior here causes bad invoices to silently reach clients.

summary.objective: Automatically generate an invoice as soon as a contract is signed, removing the manual step and delay currently involved in billing.
summary.scope: Likely: subscribe to the contract's 'signed' event, determine the applicable invoice(s) (one-shot or a billing schedule), pull amount/VAT/payment terms from the contract, create the invoice in a draft or pending state, and route it to the right person for review/issuance — while preventing duplicates and handling incomplete data.
assumptions: The invoice is created in a draft/pending state requiring finance review, not issued and sent immediately.
One signed contract maps to one invoice by default, unless milestone/recurring billing is confirmed.
The signature event is the sole trigger; no other conditions (e.g. down-payment received) gate creation.
Standard French VAT and legal invoice numbering rules apply by default.
summary.blind_spot: The request assumes 'invoice the contract' is a single, simple document creation, but a contract is one-to-many with its invoices (milestones, recurring, partial billing) and the invoice's amount/VAT/terms must come from somewhere well-defined — plus duplicate-trigger and amendment handling are unaddressed. Building this as a one-shot script when the real need is a billing schedule engine (or vice versa) would require a costly rebuild.

briefs.problem: Invoices are created manually after a contract is signed, causing delay and inconsistent billing.
briefs.solution: Automatically generate a draft invoice when a contract's status changes to 'signed'.
introduces: a contract-signature event trigger that other automation can subscribe to
an idempotency/dedup mechanism to prevent duplicate invoices on re-signature or amendment
a draft-invoice review and validation step for finance before issuance
notification routing to finance and/or the client's billing contact
a per-client/country configuration layer for billing rules and VAT treatment

challenges.headline: One contract, one invoice assumption
challenges.premise: The request implies a signed contract produces exactly one invoice.
challenges.alternative: Contracts are commonly billed via milestones, recurring subscriptions, or partial invoicing — one contract routinely emits many invoices over its life.
challenges.consequence: Building a 1:1 trigger now means re-architecting the billing logic later when the first milestone or recurring contract appears, likely with production data already flowing through it.
challenges.recommendation: Confirm with Finance whether any current or near-term contracts bill on a schedule or in installments before locking the data model.

challenges.headline: Sync-blocking vs async trigger unspecified
challenges.premise: The request treats 'when signed, create invoice' as a single mechanical step without specifying timing.
challenges.alternative: A queued, asynchronous creation (with a visible pending state and retry) is standard when downstream data (amount, VAT, billing contact) might not be ready at the exact moment of signature.
challenges.consequence: A synchronous, blocking implementation means a missing billing field fails the signature action itself in front of the user — coupling two unrelated lifecycles.
challenges.recommendation: Decide sync vs async explicitly with engineering before estimating; this changes error handling and UX, not just implementation detail.

challenges.headline: Finance gatekeeping is assumed, not confirmed
challenges.premise: The model assumes the invoice starts as 'draft' pending finance validation rather than going straight to 'issued'.
challenges.alternative: Some clients may want full automation with no manual gate, especially for simple/standard contracts — validation could be assumed rather than required.
challenges.consequence: If the review step isn't actually wanted, it re-adds the manual delay the automation was meant to remove; if it is wanted but unspecified, permissions get bolted on late (a known recurring gap in this platform).
challenges.recommendation: Confirm explicitly with Finance whether every auto-created invoice needs sign-off, or only ones flagged by exception (e.g. missing data, high amount).

complexity_reasons: billing-rule engine must handle amount sourcing, VAT and numbering correctly under legal constraints
idempotency logic needed across signature, re-signature and amendment events
sync/async trigger design affects error handling and rollback on the contract side
config layer likely needed if billing rules vary by client, contract type or country
briefs.cost_driver: the billing-rule engine that determines invoice count, amount source and numbering across one-to-many contract-to-invoice scenarios
risks: Duplicate invoices if amendment re-signature isn't excluded from the trigger
Legal numbering/VAT non-compliance if the automation issues invoices without proper sequencing safeguards
Broken or incomplete drafts if required billing fields (amount, billing contact) aren't populated at signature time
Permissions gap: if finance validation isn't built in from the start, retrofitting a review gate after go-live is expensive
The existing contract lifecycle and amendment flow will need explicit hooks to distinguish 'new contract signed' from 'amendment issued'

opportunities.text: Build the signature-event trigger and idempotency layer as a general contract-lifecycle hook, not a one-off invoice trigger.
Route invoice notifications through the Contact's role (billing) rather than a hardcoded recipient, reusing the existing contact-role model.
Generalize the draft-then-validate creation pattern for other auto-generated documents (e.g. end-of-mission invoicing).

next_steps: Confirm with Finance whether contracts bill 1:1, on milestones, or on a recurring schedule before designing the data model.
Decide synchronous vs asynchronous trigger and draft-vs-issued initial state with engineering.
Define the idempotency rule for amendments/re-signature explicitly (what counts as a duplicate trigger).
Confirm whether finance validation is mandatory for every auto-created invoice or only exception cases.

decisions.decision: Invoice creation triggers on the contract's transition to 'signed' status
Auto-created invoice starts in 'draft' status pending finance validation
decisions.why: Reduces risk of an incorrect or non-compliant invoice reaching a client fully unattended.
decisions.alternative: Issue the invoice immediately without review.
decisions.tradeoff: Adds a manual step back into the process, in exchange for lower compliance and error risk.

decisions.decision: Invoice numbering and VAT must follow French legal rules

open_decisions: Whether one contract maps to one invoice or requires milestone/recurring/partial billing support
Sync vs async trigger mechanics and error handling on incomplete data
Whether the billing rule varies by client, contract type or country
Exact idempotency rule distinguishing amendment/re-signature from a new contract signature
Who is notified and through which channel once the invoice is created

briefs.problem: Invoices are created manually after a contract is signed, causing delay and inconsistent billing data reaching clients.
briefs.solution: An automated trigger on contract signature that generates the invoice(s) from contract data, without manual intervention.
introduces: a contract-event subscription mechanism that other modules can hook into
a billing-rule engine to derive amount, VAT, numbering and payment terms from contract data
idempotency and duplicate-firing control on a lifecycle trigger
a draft/review surface for machine-generated invoices before client issuance
an audit trail linking triggering contract event to the invoice(s) it produced

challenges.headline: Signature as the sole billing trigger
challenges.premise: The request assumes 'contract signed' is the single event that should produce an invoice.
challenges.alternative: Many contracts bill on a schedule (start date, milestones, recurring subscription, partial billing) rather than at the moment of signature, and a contract is one-to-many with its invoices in this system.
challenges.consequence: Building a one-shot 'signature → invoice' automation now means re-architecting into a billing-schedule engine later once milestone or recurring contracts surface — a much larger change than adding a feature.
challenges.recommendation: Map out the actual billing patterns across the contract portfolio (one-off, milestone, recurring) with Finance before committing to a single-trigger design.

challenges.headline: Auto-created means auto-issued
challenges.premise: The request implies the invoice, once generated, is ready to go — but it doesn't say whether it reaches the client directly.
challenges.alternative: Given French VAT and legal numbering constraints, most finance teams want auto-generated invoices to land as a reviewable draft, not be issued straight-through.
challenges.consequence: A straight-through design that later needs a review gate means retrofitting an approval workflow and permission model onto an already-live financial document flow — the highest-risk kind of rework in an invoicing system.
challenges.recommendation: Confirm with Finance whether auto-created invoices require sign-off before issuance, and build the draft state in from day one regardless.

challenges.headline: One rule for every contract
challenges.premise: The automation is described as if a single rule applies uniformly to all contracts.
challenges.alternative: Billing triggers, VAT treatment and numbering can plausibly vary by client, contract type or country, as they do elsewhere on this platform.
challenges.consequence: Hardcoding a single trigger/amount rule risks a costly rebuild the first time an exception client or foreign entity is onboarded.
challenges.recommendation: Explicitly ask whether any client, contract type or country needs a different billing trigger or rule before deciding config_vs_custom.

complexity_reasons: billing-rule derivation (amount, VAT, numbering, terms) from contract data
idempotency and sync/async handling on a shared lifecycle event
review/approval surface needed for a financial document, not just data creation
briefs.cost_driver: the billing-rule engine that derives invoice amount, schedule and legal fields from contract data
risks: Duplicate invoices if the signature event (or a re-signed amendment) fires the trigger twice without idempotency protection.
Incorrect VAT or numbering reaching a client if incomplete contract data is used to auto-generate the invoice at signature time.
The existing invoicing module's permission model may not have a 'system-generated draft awaiting review' state and will need extending.
Contract amendments re-opening signature could unintentionally re-trigger invoicing unless the workflow explicitly defines this.
A synchronous design that blocks contract signing on invoice-generation failure could stall a business-critical signature action.

opportunities.text: Generalize the contract-signed event into a reusable subscription point other automations (notifications, provisioning) can hook into.
Build the billing-rule/schedule engine to handle one-to-many invoice generation (milestone, recurring, partial) rather than a single-invoice shortcut.
Extend the audit-trail approach (event → generated document) as a pattern reusable for other auto-generated financial or contractual documents.

next_steps: Confirm with Finance whether billing is truly signature-triggered and single-invoice, or schedule-based and multi-invoice per contract.
Decide whether invoice generation is synchronous (blocking signature) or asynchronous (queued with a pending state), since this drives the architecture.
Settle whether generated invoices require a review/approval step before client issuance.
Clarify whether the automation rule needs to vary by client, contract type or country before locking the design as config or hardcoded.

decisions.decision: The contract's 'signed' lifecycle event is the trigger point for invoice generation
Invoice fields (amount, VAT, numbering, payment terms) are sourced from the Contract

open_decisions: Whether one invoice or a billing-schedule of several invoices is expected per contract
Synchronous vs. asynchronous invoice creation and its error-handling model
Whether the auto-created invoice requires finance review before being issued to the client
Whether the billing rule varies by client, contract type or country
Whether an issued amendment (re-signature) should re-trigger invoice creation
Idempotency strategy to prevent duplicate invoices from repeated or concurrent trigger firing
Success metrics for the automation (time saved, error reduction, etc.)

briefs.problem: Invoices are created manually after a contract is signed, causing delay and inconsistent billing.
briefs.solution: An automated invoice-generation step triggered by contract signature, producing a draft invoice for finance review.
introduces: an event-driven trigger subscribed to the contract-signature lifecycle
a billing-rule engine deciding one-shot vs. milestone/recurring invoice generation
idempotency and duplicate-prevention handling on the signature event
a new review/validation surface for finance before issuance
per-client/contract-type configurability of the billing rule

challenges.headline: One contract, one invoice assumption
challenges.premise: The request treats 'invoice the contract' as a single document created once at signature.
challenges.alternative: A Contract is one-to-many with its Invoices — milestone billing, recurring subscriptions and partial invoicing are common, and the correct cardinality has to be confirmed per contract type before any data model is built.
challenges.consequence: Building a one-shot script when the real need is a billing schedule (or the reverse) means a costly rebuild of the trigger, the data model and the review workflow.
challenges.recommendation: Confirm with Finance whether any contract types require multiple or scheduled invoices before defining the trigger logic.

challenges.headline: Signature as sole, single-fire trigger
challenges.premise: Contract signature is assumed to be the one and only event that creates the invoice, firing exactly once.
challenges.alternative: Signature events can retry, contracts can be amended after signing (re-opening signature per the Contract/Amendment pattern), and some clients may gate invoicing on a separate condition (e.g. down payment received) rather than signature alone.
challenges.consequence: Without explicit idempotency and amendment handling, a retried event or a post-signature amendment can generate duplicate or stale invoices that reach a client unreviewed.
challenges.recommendation: Define idempotency keys and the amendment-handling behavior (re-trigger, ignore, flag for review) before implementing the listener.

challenges.headline: Uniform rule across all clients
challenges.premise: The automation is implicitly described as a single rule applying to every contract the same way.
challenges.alternative: On this configurable multi-client platform, billing rules routinely vary by client or contract type (e.g. some clients require manual approval, others auto-issue) — this is a config decision, not a universal default.
challenges.consequence: If variability surfaces after build, the hardcoded trigger has to be retrofitted into a rule engine, touching the trigger, the review workflow and permissions at once.
challenges.recommendation: Ask the client explicitly whether this rule is uniform or varies, before deciding config vs. hardcoded implementation.

complexity_reasons: billing cardinality (one-shot vs. schedule) is undecided and reshapes the data model
event-trigger idempotency and amendment re-triggering are unaddressed
review/permission surface for a financial document is undefined
potential per-client configurability adds a rule-engine layer
briefs.cost_driver: deciding and building the contract-to-invoice billing logic (single document vs. milestone/recurring schedule) and making the signature trigger idempotent and amendment-aware
risks: Duplicate invoices from a signature event firing twice or being retried.
Invoices created with incomplete or incorrect amount/VAT/terms because the data source isn't defined.
Contract amendments after signature silently bypassing or duplicating the original invoice.
Undefined permissions could let unauthorized roles see or edit a draft financial document before review.
French VAT and legal numbering rules must be correctly enforced from the first invoice generated, with no room for retrofit.

opportunities.text: Build the contract-signature listener as a general-purpose event subscription mechanism, since signature is already described as a common cross-module trigger.
Generalize the billing-schedule logic (milestone/recurring invoice generation) so it can later serve end-of-mission billing or other multi-invoice sources.
Externalize the billing rule (who gets auto-issued vs. reviewed) into client-level configuration rather than a hardcoded script.

next_steps: Confirm with Finance/client whether a signed contract always maps to one invoice or can require a billing schedule.
Define the source of amount, VAT, payment terms and currency on the invoice before building the trigger.
Decide whether the trigger is synchronous (blocking, user-facing) or asynchronous (queued, with a pending state), and design idempotency accordingly.
Specify who can view/edit/approve the auto-created invoice before issuance, and whether the rule varies by client.

decisions.decision: The invoice amount, VAT and terms will be sourced from the Contract record
New invoices are created in a draft/pending state requiring finance review rather than issued immediately
decisions.why: An auto-created financial document reaching a client unchecked is a compliance and correctness risk.
decisions.alternative: Issue and send the invoice immediately on signature with no human checkpoint.
decisions.tradeoff: Adds a review step and latency in exchange for catching bad or incomplete data before the client sees it.

open_decisions: Whether one contract produces one invoice or a billing schedule (milestone/recurring)
Whether the signature trigger is synchronous or asynchronous, and how idempotency is enforced
Who can view, edit or approve the auto-created invoice before issuance
Whether the automation rule is uniform or configurable per client/contract type
How incomplete contract data or post-signature amendments are handled
Success metrics for the automation (time saved, error reduction, etc.)
